text: Loess is a specific soil formed in arid and semi-arid regions during the Quaternary period, characterized by a distinct material composition and microscopic structure, resulting in a mantle-like accumulation based on ancient topography. The relationships between particle gradation, contact relationships, types and sizes of pores, types, states, and characteristics of cementation structure form the fundamental framework of loess, which determine its unique structure, relatively low strength, and low carrying capacity, with some exhibiting collapsibility. Loess exhibits special engineering properties that pose significant hazards to engineering structures, garnering widespread attention in the engineering field and being regarded as a special engineering geological entity. In tunnel projects, loess is considered a specific type of soft rock, categorized as weak surrounding rock of Types II and III. Loess tunnels involve excavation of loess, which significantly alters the natural stress state of the loess body during the excavation process. Following the application of initial supports and secondary linings, the secondary and tertiary stress states of the loess surrounding rock are also modified, further complicating the already intricate engineering characteristics of loess and undergoing a complex process of stress adjustment. This process transfers a portion of the surrounding rock's stress to nearby areas, while another portion is released through geological hazards such as collapses. The development of loess joints, low structural strength, and poor hydraulic properties are the primary challenges faced in loess tunnels. The engineering geological conditions of the tunnel are closely related to the excavation methods, support systems, and types of lining structures employed during construction; the engineering geological conditions of the surrounding rock serve both as the material basis for tunnel geological hazards and as a medium for studying these hazards. Excluding human factors, the origins of tunnel geological hazards can be traced back to the engineering geological conditions of the surrounding rock. The basic engineering geological characteristics of loess, such as physical and mechanical properties, compressibility, collapsibility, compaction behavior, shearing resistance, and hydrological properties, determine the engineering properties of the surrounding rock in loess tunnels. This paper systematically discusses the main geological hazards encountered during loess tunnel construction in conjunction with the engineering characteristics of loess—its meaning, genesis, composition, and structure—on this basis exploring its disaster mechanisms, including physical geological effects, seismic effects, hydrological effects, regional geological effects, comprehensive effects, and construction effects, summarizing the main types of geological hazards associated with loess tunnels. Collapse is the most common geological hazard in loess tunnels. This paper analyzes the main factors influencing tunnel collapses (geological and construction factors) and examines the mechanisms and patterns of loess tunnel collapses through mechanical methods, horizontal projection methods, numerical methods, and probabilistic methods, assessing collapse hazards. It summarizes some new characteristics of collapses in loess tunnel projects: rapid occurrence, lack of warning, intermittent and directional behavior, dependency of collapse scale on construction activities, and susceptibility to collapse at interfaces of geological changes, soil-rock interfaces, and groundwater accumulation zones. The introduction of safety system engineering concepts and probabilistic methods evaluates collapse hazards in loess tunnels, incorporating gray models to predict tunnel geological hazards. Numerical simulation analysis of the mechanical behavior and deformation characteristics during loess tunnel excavation based on the finite element method shows great promise. The influence of groundwater within loess strata on tunnels is significant, with the role of water in the surrounding rock being a key focus of research and concern during the design and construction of loess tunnels. For some large tunnel projects, in-depth studies are still needed regarding the conditions for cavity formation in saturated loess and the control techniques for loess inundation. The pores and voids in loess serve as effective storage and migration pathways for groundwater, constituting an important aquifer medium in loess. Additionally, the low-strength surrounding rock introduces new joint fissures post-excavation, further expanding groundwater pathways. This paper analyzes the sources and roles of water in tunnel surrounding rock, assessing the water hazards arising from increased water content; it also evaluates the water sensitivity of the surrounding loess rock. The effects of water in loess primarily manifest as softening, lubrication, dissolution, added weight, impact, and disintegration, resulting not only in the reduction of...

The landslide disaster of loess tunnels can be divided into four types: (1) Sliding along structural surfaces of surrounding rock: After the tunnel excavation, under the influence of various factors, a plastic zone develops in the surrounding rock, increasing the surrounding rock pressure, which triggers intense stress redistribution within the surrounding rock. This leads to the continuous propagation of joint fractures in the surrounding rock, resulting in vertical joints eventually merging to form a collapse column. Structural joints may also culminate in an oblique configuration to form a collapse wedge. When the strength (frictional force) of the loess surrounding rock is insufficient to support the weight of the collapse mass, a collapse occurs, where the former leads to a ceiling fall or top heave, and the latter forms a dome-like roof collapse. (2) Softening of surrounding rock: The strength of dry loess surrounding rock is slightly higher than that of the designed surrounding rock category. However, when subjected to water infiltration, a significant portion of molecular attraction is replaced by the adhesive force of water, leading to the erosion of some cemented structures. The structural strength of the loess rapidly declines, and its compressive and shear resistance significantly weakens. In the Xinsongshuwan tunnel, after excavation, varying degrees of water leakage were observed at different sections. Under wet conditions, the tunnel crown and side walls exhibited strong disintegration behavior, resulting in greatly reduced wall performance. The settlement of wet loess caused an increase in lateral pressure, leading to abnormal convergence deformation at the arch foot. In the Yangma River tunnel of the Shenyan Line, a significant water content was observed at the soil-rock interface, with a discharge rate of 180 m² daily, saturating the bottom of the side wall and causing deformation in the softening soil at the base. The overlying sand layer stripped and slid down, extruding the initial support, eventually leading to the continuous collapse of the loess and expanding the area of the landslide. This type of collapse is significantly influenced by the lateral pressure of loess and is exacerbated by reduced strength induced by groundwater involvement. (3) Large cross-section excavation: Large cross-section tunnels alter the previously common width-to-height ratio, with the width exceeding the height, resulting in a new pattern of stress distribution in the surrounding rock, as illustrated in Fig. 2-2. Based on the geological characteristics of loess and construction period requirements, to mitigate the risk of collapse, the design of the Xinzhongling tunnel follows the principle of short excavation. It features a single central circular cross-section with an excavation width of 13.1 m, height of 9.4 m, and area of 106.7 m². This large cross-section excavation increases construction difficulty and the probability of landslides and other hazards. (4) Complex geological conditions: The tunnel traverses combined rock strata, with abrupt changes in lithology at the strata interfaces. The layers may also contain sand layers or ancient riverbeds and depressions, leading to a complex and variable state of the surrounding rock. The surrounding rock is fragmented and loose, exhibiting very poor stability.

When constructing tunnels in geologically unfavorable sections, phenomena such as roof rock collapse, sidewall sliding, and even severe incidents like failure of the ceiling can often be encountered; these occurrences are referred to as collapses during construction. Collapses pose threats to personal safety, delay construction timelines, and lead to increased instability of the tunnel surrounding rock, thus, it is essential to pay significant attention to these issues throughout the tunnel design and construction process. There are numerous causes leading to collapses, but generally, they can be categorized into four primary factors: geological and geomorphological conditions, climatic and hydrological conditions, investigation and design, and construction methods. 

1. **Geological and Geomorphological Conditions:** Geological conditions are the fundamental factors causing collapses. Tunnels that traverse fault zones, folds, severely weathered fractured zones, or sedimentary layers are prone to collapses. The development of bedding planes, fractures, and karst caves in rock masses can create unstable hazardous rock masses. When the stress in the strata exceeds the long-term strength of these rock formations, the tunnel is highly likely to experience collapses due to accelerated deformation of the rock mass. 

2. **Climatic and Hydrological Conditions:** Natural processes such as weathering and erosion of rock masses (including atmospheric precipitation, cave water, and fracture water) as well as geological hazards like heavy rainfall, debris flows, and earthquakes are significant causes of tunnel collapses, especially the impact of hydrological conditions is particularly pronounced. Numerous cases of engineering collapses indicate that sudden heavy rainfall and prolonged overcast weather are important triggering factors for collapses. Changes in surface precipitation and groundwater levels can often disrupt the original structure of the surrounding rock, soften the rock and soil mass, and reduce their strength and bearing capacity. Additionally, if drainage is inadequate, this can lead to increased pore water pressure, reduced effective stress in the surrounding rock, and a sharp decrease in shear strength. Moreover, if there is a large variation in groundwater flow in sandy soil, excessive hydraulic gradient can lead to dynamic hydraulic effects that exacerbate soil erosion or sand flow phenomena, triggering collapse incidents.

3. **Investigation and Design:** Geological investigation is a crucial phase in the tunnel construction process. Inadequate geological exploration and insufficient understanding of the main strata and geological structures within the tunnel area can lead to an inaccurate reflection of the actual engineering geological conditions of the tunnel, severely impacting the determination of the tunnel design scheme and construction methods.

4. **Construction Methods:** Construction is the most direct cause of tunnel collapses. Currently, in China, the number of construction units is vast, and the technical level of highway tunnel construction teams is quite uneven, with varying management and construction standards. Additionally, some operational procedures in certain construction phases are not standardized, and some contractors or personnel lack in-depth study, understanding, research, and application of the New Austrian Tunneling Method principles, resulting in widespread non-standard construction practices.

The construction of tunnels in karst development areas has long been a challenging issue for the engineering community. In particular, the construction of shallow-buried tunnels in karst regions is highly impacted by factors such as karst features, groundwater, and shallow geological structures, resulting in frequent geological hazards during construction. However, due to the complexity of the shallow-buried karst geological conditions, there is currently a lack of research outcomes related to geological hazards in karst tunnels, which severely lags behind the requirements of tunnel construction development. The Yunwu Mountain Tunnel section of the Suining-Yu Highway, which is under construction, has a length of 450 meters of shallow-buried karst; the entire section is characterized by developed karst and abundant groundwater; the surrounding rock is significantly affected by geological structures, making the geological situation extremely complex. During construction, there were three major collapses (including one roof fall), four instances of mud inflow, and six occurrences of water burst; surface phenomena such as sinkholes, cracks, loss of water in fields, and building cracks were observed multiple times. This paper relies on the key project "Stability and Reliability of Tunnel and Underground Space Engineering Structures (50334060)" and the research topic "Study on Information-Based Construction Monitoring Technology of Yunwu Mountain Tunnel on Suining-Yu Highway" to analyze geological hazard incidents that occurred during the construction of the shallow-buried karst section of the Yunwu Mountain Tunnel; it examines the disaster mechanisms of geological hazards in shallow-buried karst segments; investigates disaster prediction and prevention measures for shallow-buried karst tunnels; and analyzes the causes and management processes of major roof falls in the shallow-buried karst section. The following key conclusions are drawn: ① Based on the occurrences of geological hazards in the shallow-buried karst segment, the disaster mechanisms are analyzed, revealing that the main contributing factors include karst caverns, water, geological structure, and engineering factors. ② Geological hazards in shallow-buried karst segments exhibit not only the characteristics of geological hazards in karst and shallow tunnels but also possess high suddenness, large impact scope, and rapid evolution characteristics. ③ A synthetic prediction and prevention system for karst geological hazards is proposed, incorporating advanced geological forecasting, preliminary support, and monitoring measurement technologies. By utilizing advanced geological forecasting information, deficiencies in geological survey data are compensated for, providing a basis for the selection of preliminary support measures; the analysis results from monitoring measurement data are used to modify the designs of advanced and initial support. These three elements form an interrelated and continuously optimizing system. ④ Analysis of the occurrence and development of the significant roof fall in the shallow-buried karst segment indicates that the collapse was primarily caused by geological structures, karst mud inflow, and water, with the influx of surface river water into the collapse area being the main factor leading to the expansion of the collapse scale. ⑤ Finite element calculation results align with monitoring measurement results, confirming the rationality of the treatment plan for the collapse disaster. Areas of high stress in the lining structure are located at the left and right shoulder positions, followed by the crown, while the lower arch experiences less stress, with significant settlement occurring due to the absence of intermediate supports and temporary arches during the sequential excavation process. ⑥ Simulation analysis results show that after backfilling the collapse area, no yielding zones appeared at the arch crown, which is largely consistent with the monitoring measurement results. This indicates that the reinforcement and densification of the support system effectively improved the stress conditions of the supporting structure.

Collapse refers to sudden failures such as land subsidence, heap collapse, and rockfall caused by the instability of surrounding rock, which are destructive geological hazards. These phenomena frequently occur in fault rupture zones, expansive rocks and soils, Quaternary loose rock formations, unconformity contact surfaces, contact zones of intrusive rocks, and sections where unfavorable combinations of rock mass structural planes exist. It is one of the most common hazard occurrences in tunnel construction. Statistics show that local destabilization of surrounding rock accounts for 36% of total collapses in Class III surrounding rock, 62% in Class IV surrounding rock, and 75% in Class V surrounding rock. This indicates a close relationship between the occurrence of collapse and the structural characteristics of the rock mass. For example, between 1984 and 1997 in Japan, 26% of the 220 fatalities in tunnel construction resulted from collapses; the Gotthard Base Tunnel (14.6 km) between Italy and Switzerland experienced 25 deaths due to collapse; 25% of the 415 tunnels constructed in the Chengdu-Kunming Railway experienced large-scale collapses; the Liangfengya Tunnel (4.27 km) along the Chuanqian Line experienced significant pressure leading to large-scale collapses at the arch due to water-induced swelling of fault clay, resulting in cracking of the arch ring and side walls; in the Xiping Tunnel (0.298 km) of the Daqin Line, a single collapse reached a volume of 9,000 m³, penetrating the surface; the Kao Chair Mountain Tunnel experienced a severe "skyward" collapse, creating a massive sinkhole measuring 71 m long, 51 m wide, and 21 m deep, with an estimated collapse volume of around 20,000 m³, forming a collapse body extending up to 188 m within the tunnel. The Hongmiao Tunnel on the Chengdu-Kunming Line was forced to change routes due to significant inflow and severe collapses.

Cave-ins refer to phenomena encountered during tunnel construction in geologically unstable sections, such as the collapse of overburden, sidewall sliding, and even severe incidents like roof falls, collectively termed as cave-ins in construction terms. The instability leading to tunnel cave-ins comprises both the failure of the surrounding rock and the failure of the support structures. Ultimately, tunnel cave-ins stem from insufficient understanding of the stability of the surrounding rock and the principles of the shotcrete and anchorage support structures.

The manifestations of cave-ins during tunnel construction are diverse, including collapse of karst caves, mining-induced subsidence in coal seams or old pits, the failure of dangerous rock bodies, and the collapse of fault zones. The causes of tunnel cave-ins are multifaceted, arising from geological sudden factors, human factors, and lack of experience. In summary, the primary causes can be categorized as follows: 

1. Geological Factors: Although there are various reasons for cave-ins, geological factors are decisive. Thus, enhancing geological work during construction is fundamental to avoiding and preventing cave-in accidents.

2. Design Factors: Inappropriate selection of excavation portal locations, such as positioning within large sliding bodies or faults, can trigger portal cave-ins; inadequately designed support parameters may fail to ensure stability of the surrounding rock during the period from excavation to secondary lining; improper treatment measures in the design for especially adverse geological segments.

3. Construction Technical Factors: Inadequate adaptation of construction methods to geological conditions or changes in geological conditions without implementing corresponding measures, such as rock classification, often leads to discrepancies between actual excavation conditions and design; failure to timely apply the crown arch in weak surrounding rock results in the lack of a closed ring structure to withstand stresses; issues in blasting design may cause excessive disturbance to the surrounding rock; in the New Austrian Tunneling Method, lack of timely and adequate measurement work or delayed information feedback may result in decision-making errors.

4. Management Factors: During tunnel construction, failure to strictly adhere to design documents, construction organization specifications, and related requirements can lead to significant issues.

Tunneling collapses can be categorized into different types based on various criteria such as scale, location, mechanism, and form. (1) Gravity collapse type. This type of collapse primarily occurs due to self-weight stress causing the surrounding rock to slide or fall along weak structural planes, resulting in minor scale collapses. (2) Fragmentation and loosening type. When the stress on the surrounding rock exceeds its yield strength and the surrounding rock belongs to a fractured rock mass, the surrounding rock may dislocate along structural surfaces, forming a fragmented and loosened zone. The loosened material itself is unstable, and under the influence of groundwater and other factors, it can easily lead to instability of the tunnel walls or collapse of the arch. If not promptly mitigated, this type of collapse may lead to a series of failures, resulting in severe accidents. (3) Tensile failure type. When the concentrated tensile stress generated at the tunnel crown exceeds the tensile strength of the surrounding rock, tensile failure of the rock mass occurs, leading to collapse. (4) Bending and bulging type. This form of failure is common in layered surrounding rock. (5) Shear slip type. When the surrounding rock at the tunnel crown experiences high concentrated compressive stress and the rock mass has a blocky or thick layered structure, this type of failure frequently occurs in the tunnel surrounding rock.

Karst refers to the various geological processes, forms, and phenomena occurring in soluble rock due to the erosive action of water. Soluble rocks include sulfate rocks (such as mirabilite and gypsum), carbonate rocks (such as dolomite and limestone), and halide rocks (such as rock salt). Among these, carbonate rocks are the most widely distributed soluble rocks in our country. Under the prolonged action of groundwater, fractured rock masses gradually form karst rock masses. The interaction of groundwater causes the fractures in soluble rocks, as well as the pores within the rock, to evolve into dissolution voids, solution cavities, and sinkholes, which may even develop into large dissolution ponds, subterranean rivers, and a network of karst conduits. These voids provide conditions for the storage of karst water bodies and exhibit different storage types, specifically categorized as follows: 

1. Fracture karst water storage type. Under the erosive action of flowing groundwater, the widths of joint and bedding fractures in soluble rocks are gradually eroded wider, forming a fracture karst water storage type.

2. Solution cavity and pond water storage type. The solution cavities and ponds hidden within the rock mass often serve as a large reservoir of static water in regions with strong karst development. When surface water flows and subterranean rivers are present nearby and connect with the solution cavities, a vast karst water body replenishment system is formed.

3. Fault karst water storage type. Faults often contain a large amount of fractured rock, interconnecting with many fracture water pathways, thus possessing significant storage space and capacity. Particularly in active faults, derived structural zones and fabric structure zones frequently form fault aquifer structures.

4. Subterranean river and karst conduit water storage type. Over long geological eras, due to the erosive action of groundwater, the fracture zones and layers within horizontal flow zones can develop into large-scale underground rivers and networks of karst conduits for water storage.

5. Interbedded karst water storage type. The interbedded karst water storage type is somewhat similar to the fault karst water storage type; however, the thickness of these soluble rock layers is generally not substantial, and the rock types forming the aquiclude are mostly sandstone, shale, etc., making it easier to develop water-rich areas nearby.

6. Combined karst water storage type.

Tunnels, as significant structures, are widely utilized in highway engineering in the "three high and four active" southwestern region. However, during construction in extremely complex geological environments, they inevitably encounter typical underground engineering geological hazards such as collapses, rock bursts, large deformations, and water inrushes, which severely constrain safe and efficient construction, increasing economic costs and management pressures. Conducting risk assessments for tunnel geological hazards and implementing effective emergency response measures are of vital importance for disaster prevention and mitigation in tunnel projects. 

Based on research on the engineering geological conditions of the southwestern mountainous area, this paper analyzes the types and characteristics of geological hazards encountered during the construction of highway tunnels in this region. On this basis, the Mudashan Tunnel, a representative deeply buried long tunnel in the southwestern mountainous area, is selected as the research subject. The geological hazards during the construction process of this tunnel are identified, and the risk categories are determined. Subsequently, a risk evaluation index system for geological hazards in tunnel construction is established, and the weight of the indices is determined. A risk evaluation model based on index scoring methods is constructed, and this model is used to evaluate the geological hazard risks of the Mudashan Tunnel. Finally, emergency response measures for geological hazards in mountainous highway tunnels are proposed.

The main conclusions of this paper are as follows: 
(1) The southwestern region is located at the southeastern edge of the Eurasian Plate, adjacent to the Pacific Plate and the Indian Plate. Significant active faults are developed within the area, with strong neotectonic movements and frequent seismic activities, which are structurally controlled and lead to the formation of longitudinal canyon landforms. Most engineering structures traversing this area are tunnels. The extremely complex geological environment is a core issue faced during tunnel construction in the southwestern mountainous region and is also an intrinsic factor that causes collapses, rock bursts, water inrushes, and large deformations in tunnel construction. 

(2) The Mudashan Tunnel is situated at the junction of the Songpan-Ganzi orogenic belt and the western edge of the Yangtze Platform, crossing 17 major faults, with engineering geological characteristics such as significant burial depth, substantial lithological variation, complex geological structures, and developed groundwater. These geological characteristics result in a high risk level for water inrushes and large deformations, with high risk levels for collapses and rock bursts in the construction of the Mudashan Tunnel.

(3) Based on the risk characteristics of highway tunnels in the southwestern mountainous area, a risk assessment method for tunnels in this region is established, namely the index scoring method. The evaluation index system of the index scoring method comprises 18 scoring indicators categorized into geological factors, design factors, and construction factors. By determining the indicator weights, this method is applied to assess the risks of large deformations, collapses, water inrushes, and rock bursts in the Mudashan Tunnel, yielding evaluation conclusions that correspond to the types and levels of hazards that occurred during its construction. This indicates that the method is applicable for safety risk assessment of mountain highway tunnel construction.

(4) Based on clarifying the mechanisms and manifestation characteristics of collapses, rock bursts, water inrushes, and large deformations, the phases of emergency rescue for tunnel engineering geological hazards are divided into: preparation phase, implementation phase, and rescue summary phase. In conjunction with the entire process of sudden incidents—pre-event, event, during-event, and post-event—emergency response ideas and a categorized and graded emergency management mechanism are proposed for preemptive prevention, emergency rescue, and later disposal.

(5) For sudden geological hazards such as collapses, rock bursts, and water inrushes, it is proposed that the preemptive prevention phase adhere to the principle of "investigate before excavation." For gradual geological hazards characterized by large deformations, emphasis is placed on emergency preparedness, utilizing diverse monitoring means, and timely warnings based on monitoring results.

Classification of collapses occurring during tunnel construction has not yet established specific limits regarding their scale. Generally, collapses can be categorized into three types: first, small-scale collapses, which typically range approximately 3 to 5 meters in height and width, with a volume of less than 50 cubic meters per event. Second, medium-scale collapses, roughly 1 to 2 times the diameter of the tunnel, with a volume exceeding 50 cubic meters but less than 500 cubic meters. Finally, large-scale collapses, which exceed 2 times the tunnel diameter, with a volume greater than 500 cubic meters, potentially leading to surface subsidence. For different scales of collapse events, appropriate emergency response and safety measures must be implemented to ensure the smooth progress of construction and the safety of workers. Additionally, based on the location of occurrence, collapses can be further divided into crown collapses, wall collapses, and face collapses.

Signs of Tunnel Collapse: 

Face Signs: Depending on the type of surrounding rock and groundwater conditions, the tunnel face at risk of collapse may exhibit the following situations: After hard rock excavation, continuous falling of surrounding rock fragments; after soft rock excavation, notable deformation and bulging; in sandy strata, intermittent quicksand leading to the gradual formation of a continuous inflow; increasing water discharge points are visible at the face, sudden influx of water, or increased water pressure, with water clarity deteriorating from clear to turbid.

Support Signs: If there is a potential risk of tunnel collapse, it will significantly impact the support structure, manifested as widespread cracking or spalling of shotcrete, even leading to collapse; twisting and deformation of steel supports; bulging of side wall supports at the midpoint; and significant deformation or misalignment at the connections between steel supports. Noises may occur when the support structure is subjected to excessive pressure.

Signs at Tunnel Portal and Shallow-Buried Sections: Several signs can be observed, firstly, the appearance of numerous cracks on the surface, with increasing width and quantity, indicating changes in the underground structure. Secondly, noticeable ground subsidence, usually exceeding 300 mm, with this subsidence trend continuing to intensify. These signs may result from groundwater flow, geological structural changes, or issues during construction, indicating a potential threat to tunnel stability. Therefore, upon detecting these signs, timely monitoring and response measures should be implemented to ensure safe construction and use of the tunnel.

Drilling Signs: During drilling, the drill bit may become stuck, the drilling speed may suddenly increase or obstruct, or the water quality in the borehole may become abnormally turbid.

Monitoring Data Signs: Long-term deformation not stabilizing with a consistently high deformation rate, even when measurement data curves appear stable, a sudden increase in deformation may again occur.

Factors Influencing Tunnel Collapse: The surrounding rock features fragmented, loose rock masses with weak cohesion, typically consisting of soil layers, fully weathered rock strata, or crushed zones. The structural integrity of these formations is compromised, resulting in relatively low bonding strength between the rock masses. Upon excavation of the tunnel, such rock types rely solely on friction between particles and weak cementation to form an arch structure. This type of rock mass is extremely unstable, particularly in shallow burying sections, making it susceptible to collapse. Therefore, during tunnel construction, special attention must be paid to such surrounding rock, and appropriate support and reinforcement measures should be implemented to ensure the safety of the construction and operation of the tunnel. Representative soft rock formations, such as shale, mudstone, schist, carbonaceous rock, and phyllite, exhibit insufficient strength and are prone to softening upon exposure to water, resulting in poor stability post-excavation and susceptibility to weathering and softening. Particularly in deeply buried sections, these layers experience high stress and are prone to plastic deformation, leading to internal tunnel squeezing. In shallow buried areas or segments affected by lateral pressure, the inability to form stable arch structures may result in localized collapses unless adequate reinforcement measures are taken. In regions subjected to lateral pressure, the support structures may endure significant uneven loading, potentially causing initial support to develop longitudinal cracks or deformations during construction, and in severe cases, even collapse.

Groundwater is one of the main factors prompting the occurrence and development of various geological hazards. The groundwater in fractures plays a crucial role in the formation of entrance landslides and collapses within tunnels. Fractured water exists within sandstone and limestone, where the pressure of the groundwater leads to the wedging effect along the fractures, reducing the cohesion of the rock mass, decreasing the internal friction angle, and lowering mechanical strength, thereby causing collapses. The lithology of the strata has a decisive effect on the occurrence of geological hazards during construction in windy areas and portal tunnels, especially concerning landslides, which are primarily related to lithology. To date, over 90% of collapses occur in carbonaceous slate, where the slate layers act as weak layers that easily expand, soften, and exhibit low permeability when wet, forming favorable sliding surfaces that lead to layer-parallel sliding. Additionally, since structural planes are well-developed, the shear strength of the rock mass is lower, making collapses easily occur. In limestone and sandstone, the phenomenon mainly manifests as rock fall, with collapses occurring only in localized sections. The major rock type for layered structural rock masses is carbonaceous slate. The obvious combination of joints in the surrounding rock transforms the rock mass into a polyhedron, combined with the layering of the strata, leading to the integrity of the rock mass being compromised, resulting in blocky, fragmented, angular, or mylonitic structures. Rocks modified by tectonic processes are prone to weathering, becoming unusually loose and fragmented, decreasing shear strength and stability, making collapses easy during tunnel excavation, with landslides likely at the tunnel entrances. Fault zones also serve as concentrated drainage areas for groundwater, which facilitates the occurrence and development of collapses. Moreover, the damage to the integrity of the carbonate rock mass by faults and joints provides pathways for rainwater infiltration and groundwater seepage, promoting karst development. The support form for the surrounding rock in tunnels is determined by the type of surrounding rock. However, due to the influence of groundwater, the stability factors of the rock mass after excavation are not solely determined by the surrounding rock type; the role of groundwater becomes a primary factor affecting the stability of the rock mass. In the ascending line section K256+740, the strength of the intact rock mass reaches category IV surrounding rock standards, but due to the influence of groundwater, even well-supported initial linings according to category III surrounding rock standards have experienced collapses.

Ground collapse and subsidence are common geological hazards that generally occur throughout the tunnel construction process and may still happen even after construction completion. Ground collapse is primarily caused by prolonged water inflow and significant extraction of groundwater from the tunnel. Additionally, part of the ground collapse may result from the falling or caving of the tunnel's overhead. Ground subsidence typically occurs during the initial phase of deep-buried tunnel construction. The hazards caused by ground collapse and subsidence pose significant challenges to tunnel construction and can damage surface structures, leading to the deterioration of the surrounding ecological environment. Many loose and fractured surrounding rocks can experience tunnel collapse, and generally, stability and strength treatment of the surrounding rock mass is necessary. Common treatment methods during construction include advanced long pipe sheds and advanced anchor rods, which can stabilize and enhance the strength of the surrounding rock and reduce the likelihood of tunnel collapse. For large cross-section tunnels during excavation, a stepwise excavation approach must be applied to weak surrounding rock areas, which can significantly shorten the exposure time of the surrounding rock. After excavation, immediate support treatment can be carried out, greatly increasing the stability of the tunnel surrounding rock.

During the construction of tunnel engineering, the geological structure in which it is located is often in an unstable state, which can easily lead to folding in areas of thin rock layers. If tunneling occurs in such rock formations, there is a high likelihood of weak rock mass collapse or uneven ground subsidence. When the tunnel traverses fault zones and fractured rock areas, the potential geological stresses within the rock mass are released. Due to the loose geological structure, the pressure exerted continues to increase, and coupled with the weak cementation between particles in the rock layers, the surrounding rock mass fails to maintain adequate stability post-excavation, leading to collapse phenomena. This problem is particularly pronounced when the tunnel passes through or near its entrance and exit, making it even more difficult to maintain the stability of the surrounding rock, thus increasing the likelihood of collapses. Such collapses significantly undermine the quality of tunnel construction, raise the probability of unnecessary accidents, and pose serious threats to human safety. 

To effectively prevent and mitigate the occurrence of collapses, a field investigation of the tunnel and its surrounding areas should be conducted by professionals prior to construction. This includes the collection and organization of relevant data to accurately locate potential collapse sites. Through thorough observation and analysis, a tunneling construction plan that meets the actual construction requirements should be formulated to minimize or avoid the occurrence of collapses. Additionally, reinforcement measures for the tunnel should be enhanced by employing various effective methods, such as using rock bolts for pre-support or selecting high-performance grout injection techniques to achieve reinforcement effects. If large-scale collapses of the surrounding rock mass occur during tunnel construction or within sections of the tunnel, appropriate explosives should be utilized for blasting or long tube advanced support, with strict adherence to construction procedures for excavating guide tunnels along the side walls. Corresponding measures should also be implemented to reinforce and maintain the tunnel, such as erecting steel framework, thereby addressing and preventing collapse phenomena, enhancing the safety and quality of tunnel construction, and providing robust assurances for the safety of public travel.

During tunnel construction, certain geological structures are not very stable, increasing the risk of landslides, particularly in areas with groundwater influx. During the drilling of these sections, uneven subsidence can easily occur, directly affecting construction progress and compromising tunnel structure integrity. This can significantly reduce the overall stability of the structure and shorten the tunnel's lifespan, especially at the entrance and exit points where landslides are more likely to occur. Landslides are a common hazard during tunnel construction, necessitating the implementation of effective control measures to mitigate such disasters. Prior to construction, thorough investigations of the geological structure and characteristics of the construction area are essential, along with the collection of relevant geological data. This enables real-time monitoring of specific geological conditions, facilitating informed decision-making and the adoption of effective controls to minimize the occurrence of landslides during tunnel construction, thereby ensuring the successful completion of the project.

During the construction process, coinciding with a period of frequent heavy rainfall, significant surface water infiltration occurred at the northern entrance of the tunnel. Due to the lubricating effect of the water, jointed rock layers slipped, resulting in a translational landslide at the northern section outside the tunnel. This triggered a substantial collapse in the central-northern part of the tunnel, creating a large pit approximately 80 m long, 60 m wide, and 25 m deep at the surface, with an estimated volume of around 20,000 m³. Inside the tunnel, a collapse zone extended 188 m. The collapse moved approximately 60 m towards the tunnel entrance and about 100 m towards the exit, with approximately 60 m completely obstructed by the slide debris. 

On-site investigations revealed that the depression was funnel-shaped, oriented east-west, and nearly perpendicular to the tunnel's axis, tilting towards the northern end at an angle of 70° to 80°, with a width ranging from 8.0 to 13.5 m, narrowing from top to bottom, and exhibiting steep sides. According to the geological data available, the collapse material is situated within a dissolution feature in a zone of extensional faulting. The fill consists of clayey silt derived from weathered limestone, containing a small amount of fragmented rock, with a high water content and a soft, plastic consistency, resulting in very low shear strength. Additionally, the infiltration of rainwater further created a liquefied state, significantly compromising the stability of the area. With the tunnel excavation crown buried approximately 70 m deep, the substantial overburden pressure led to initial support failure, allowing large volumes of mud and water to breach the tunnel roof and inundate the interior, culminating in a major collapse event.

This article addresses the water inflow and collapse incident occurring in the F4 fault fracture zone traversed by the Lianhuo Expressway (GZ45) Wuqiaoling Tunnel. It outlines the conditions of the water inflow and collapse, and the corresponding engineering measures taken, leading to the following conclusions: (1) The F4 fault fracture zone primarily consists of crushed rock, conglomerate, mylonite, and fault clay, with the rock mass being cemented by calcareous mud and exhibiting poor cementation. (2) The water inflow and mud outburst-induced collapse in the Wuqiaoling Tunnel crossing the F4 fault fracture zone is categorized as a debris flow disaster triggered by the fault-fractured zone. Prior to tunneling, the rock mass's strength was diminished due to factors such as hydrostatic erosion and pore water pressure. Post-excavation, it was further affected by excavation unloading, hydrodynamic erosion from groundwater, and the impact from the mixed outburst, leading to water inflow and subsequently, the collapse incident. (3) In response to the outburst and collapse conditions, several engineering measures were implemented, including sealing the face of the tunnel, drilling drainage holes, injecting grout to stabilize the collapse zone, pumping concrete to backfill the collapse cavity, reinforcing the tunnel surrounding rock with grouting, and optimizing construction methods, which yielded effective results.

In response to the collapse issues encountered at the Youfangping Tunnel across a fault segment in the southern section of the Chang-Kun High-Speed Railway, a summary of the macro deformation evolution history of the tunnel has been compiled. This analysis explores the influencing factors of collapses in water-rich soft rock tunnels that cross faults and the instability and failure modes of surrounding rock. Based on this analysis, targeted mitigation measures are proposed. The results indicate that the synergistic effect of geological structures and groundwater is the most significant factor influencing the tunnel collapse. Atmospheric precipitation has contributed to the formation of the collapse to some extent. The effects of construction methods and support measures were highlighted as a result of inadequate human understanding, while a combination of multiple factors facilitated the occurrence of this collapse. The instability model of the surrounding rock in water-rich soft rock tunnels across fault zones can be summarized as a synergistic destruction model combining bending, bulging, and bedding slip. Based on the analysis of the tunnel collapse mechanism, integrated mitigation measures both inside and outside the tunnel have been proposed, demonstrating strong effectiveness and relevance in fundamentally preventing future collapses. This research outcome can provide engineering analogs for the design and construction of water-rich soft rock tunnels traversing faults, holding significant engineering relevance.
